era_context: The War of Austrian Succession (1740–1748) continues to dominate European politics; the Jacobite rising in Scotland represents the last major attempt to restore the Stuart dynasty; the Qing Dynasty consolidates control over China and expands westward; the Ottoman Empire faces continued pressure from European powers.

events.title: Jacobite Rising of 1745 begins
events.description: On 19 August 1745, Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie), the grandson of the deposed James II, landed in Scotland and raised the Jacobite standard at Glenfinnan, initiating the last major attempt to restore the Stuart dynasty to the British throne. The rising attracted significant Highland support and initially achieved military successes, including the capture of Edinburgh and a victory at Prestonpans on 21 September 1745. However, the rising ultimately failed due to lack of French support, limited English support, and the superior organisation of government forces. The campaign would culminate in the decisive defeat at Culloden in April 1746.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts and military records; The Lyon in Mourning (Robert Forbes, compiled 1747–1775)

events.certainty_note: The date of the landing and the raising of the standard are precisely documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Battle of Fontenoy
events.description: On 11 May 1745, French forces under Marshal Saxe defeated the Allied army (British, Dutch, Austrian, Hanoverian) at Fontenoy in the War of Austrian Succession. This was a major French victory that demonstrated the effectiveness of Saxe's tactical innovations and secured French control of the Spanish Netherlands. The battle is famous for the alleged courtesy exchange between British and French soldiers before combat ('Gentlemen of the French Guard, fire first!'—though this is likely apocryphal). The victory boosted French morale and contributed to French territorial gains in the eventual peace settlement.
key_figures: William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland (Allied commander)
King George II (British monarch, present at the battle)

sources.name: Saxe's Reveries upon the Art of War

events.certainty_note: Date and outcome are well-documented in military records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Battle of Prestonpans
events.description: On 21 September 1745, Jacobite forces under Lord George Murray defeated the British government army commanded by Sir John Cope at Prestonpans, near Edinburgh. This was a significant early victory for the Jacobite rising and demonstrated the effectiveness of Highland tactics against conventional infantry. The victory boosted Jacobite morale and contributed to the capture of Edinburgh, though it ultimately did not translate into sustained military success. The battle is notable for the rapid Highland charge that broke the British lines.
events.certainty_note: Date and outcome are well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's southern tour
events.description: In 1745, the Qianlong Emperor undertook his first major southern tour of the Qing Empire, visiting the Yangtze River region and southern provinces. These tours were important for imperial legitimacy and for gathering information about local conditions. The tours also served to reinforce Qing control over the southern regions and to demonstrate imperial benevolence. Qianlong would undertake six major southern tours during his reign, making them a characteristic feature of his rule.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The imperial tours are well-documented in Qing records.

events.title: Russo-Swedish War (1741–1743) aftermath and peace consolidation
events.description: In 1745, the aftermath of the Russo-Swedish War (1741–1743) was being consolidated. Russia had gained territory in the Baltic and Finland, and Sweden had lost significant power. The peace settlement (Treaty of Åbo, 1743) had established a new balance of power in the Baltic region, with Russia emerging as the dominant power. By 1745, the consequences of this shift were becoming clear, with Sweden's decline as a major power and Russia's rise as a Baltic hegemon.

sources.name: Treaty of Åbo (1743) and diplomatic records

events.certainty_note: The peace settlement and its consequences are well-documented.

events.title: Nadir Shah's campaigns in the Caucasus and Central Asia
events.description: In 1745, Nadir Shah of Persia continued his military campaigns in the Caucasus and Central Asia, seeking to expand Persian power and consolidate control over these regions. These campaigns were part of Nadir Shah's broader imperial expansion and represented a reassertion of Persian power after Ottoman and Russian encroachments. The campaigns were largely successful in the short term but contributed to the destabilisation of the region.
events.certainty_note: The campaigns occurred in this period; exact dating of specific operations is less certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jacobite rising of 1745 is often portrayed as a romantic, doomed attempt to restore the Stuarts, but modern scholarship emphasises that it was also a serious military and political threat that required significant government resources to suppress. The 'Highland charge' at Prestonpans is sometimes romanticised, but recent military historians argue that the victory was due to superior tactics and terrain knowledge rather than any inherent superiority of Highland warriors. Additionally, the claim that the rising lacked 'French support' is complicated by the fact that France did provide some covert support, though not the full-scale invasion that the Jacobites hoped for.
historiographic_note: 1745 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for Britain, France, and the War of Austrian Succession. The Jacobite rising is extensively documented in both British government records and Jacobite accounts, though the latter are often written from exile and may be biased. Asian sources (Qing records, Persian chronicles) are less visible in Western archives but are well-preserved in their original languages. Indigenous perspectives on European expansion in the Americas are largely absent from contemporary European documentation.

graph_edges.note: Jacobite rising led to the Battle of Prestonpans
Both are major battles in the War of Austrian Succession
Jacobite rising culminated in the Battle of Culloden
